So now, writing hot copy faster, why do we want to do that? Because if I write 12 packages a year, I'm going to make 50% more than if I only write 6. So speeding up the writing process without allowing the quality of your copy to suffer is very important. So what are the steps? We've got several different, I forget how many, a whole bunch. So let's jump right in. We just talked about the preparation process. We're not going back there, okay? So don't worry about that. But if you go through the process I just described to you, you'll never see an empty page, you'll never have the blank page blues we used to call it. You'll never be sitting there staring off into space wondering what to do instead of actually doing and writing. So that's going to save you a lot of time. I developed this approach to copywriting. In the old days I used to just jump right in. I'd have the meeting with the client. I would then hang up the phone and I'd have a whole bunch of ideas, of course, from the phone call. And I'd just jump right in and I'd just start writing. And then inevitably I would get to some point in the copy where I'd say, I don't have enough to write here. There's more I need here. And so I'd have to leave that blank and hopefully move on to something else while the client or someone else found that material for me. So when I found this more structured approach, and I'm impossible to structure, I have no discipline whatsoever. When I found this it was just so easy to follow because it made copywriting so simple. I'm known as one of the fastest writers around. I mean, I'll write a 20 page sales page in one day. But prior to that day of writing, I've probably had a week of planning, preparing, outlining, researching, and so forth. So again, it's huge in terms of saving you time. Sleep on it. Thomas Edison was famous for taking power naps. He would be working in his laboratory and come to a place where he couldn't solve a particular problem. He'd just go into his office, shut the door, and lay down on the couch, take a nap. When he woke up, more often than not, he had a solution to the problem. I play Scrabble a lot on my iPad. So does Wendy. We're just like, she's probably playing her right now. Did I catch you? So anyway, there'll be all of the, many times I'll have I-E-O-U-D, something like that. I can't come up with a word that'll make it work. I'll stare at it, stare at it, stare at it, and then she'll holler at me to go do something, take out trash or whatever. And I'll go do something and come back. And as plain as an oz on my face, there's the word. So it's that disengagement process that allows your brain to solve the problem for you. You can't just do it by straining. I'm going to fix this or I'm going to come up with an idea here. Sometimes that break is exactly what you need. If I'm struggling with something that's complex or very difficult for me, I'll pretty print a copy out and I'll read it just before I go to sleep. And all sleep is is your conscious mind zones out and your subconscious mind starts sorting and filing all of the things that happened that day, all the thoughts you had, all of the things that happened to you. And during that process, it quite often will connect your problem with something else in your memory banks. And so then when you wake up in the morning, bang, there's a solution. It's almost miraculous. It doesn't happen every single time, but it happens often enough to really make it worth doing. Woo. Okay. There we go. All right. So I came to this one. Huh? Yeah. Get the mic. What you said about the sleeping on it was also very helpful is keeping a piece of paper or a notebook or something next to your bed. The same thing. I'm shaking my head going, oh, I'm totally with you. The same thing happens. You go to bed or you can lay down and you say, here's the problem I have. You told me this. It's like, where you going to sleep? Say, this is the problem I have. This is the solution. I need a headline for this. Yeah. And then you go take a nap, go to sleep. And while you're sleeping, have some paper nearby because sometime in the middle of the night, the idea comes to you and it's crystal clear. And if you make the mistake of saying, oh, this is great. When I wake up, I'm going to remember this. You're screwed. It's not going to happen. You just, and I've learned to just almost stay asleep almost, have a little night like to turn on real quick and I just sort of jot down whatever the idea. And sometimes in the morning I look at it and I'm like, what the heck did I write? So you got to make a secret. The words still make sense. Right. But a lot of times, you know, that idea is like it says crystallize in your brain at that time and you just write it down. Next morning you pick it up from there and you finish off the company. Last night when I went to sleep, the last thing I thought about was this campaign we're in right now. We have very, it's an online campaign. We have very high response rates. But our average sale is low. Not enough people are taking the two-year offer. Right. So that's the last thing I thought about when I went to sleep last night. When I woke up in that twilight state that you're talking about, I woke up and it just came to me, you know, change your two-year offer to three years for the price of two. Okay. Now what's that going to do? Well, first thing, I get to tell everyone who's already bought two years that we're going to give them an extra year free. That's great bonding. They're going to love this editor because we did that. Secondly, it's going to take all of the people who have bought one year and it were no longer part of my prospect pool and put them right back in the prospect pool because now I can try to pitch them the two years for three or three years for two offer. Right. And then finally, it'll raise my average unit of sale on the current promotion. So you know, I just wish I had come up with it consciously because I'd be thinking I was brilliant. The rain served it up automatically, you know, as I slept. So I learned this one when I got single after my first marriage and I wanted to meet women. I was working a regular workday just like everyone else going to work at eight o'clock in the morning and, you know, work until four or five in the afternoon and then, you know, coming home and then I got single and I decided, you know, I live on a beach. I should be out meeting women, you know, there are women on the beach or women in the bars. There's women everywhere around here and I'm not meeting them because, you know, I'm not out there when they are during the day. So I decided I was going to start going to bed at eight o'clock at night on weeknights but getting up at four in the morning so I could put in a full day by noon. And I figured at the time it would have a collateral benefit but it really had nothing to do with the decision. It was more about women. But the collateral benefit is the phone doesn't ring between four o'clock in the morning and say eight or nine. So I started doing that 22 years ago, 23 years ago and it's a habit I still have today. And what's surprising about it is that, you know, not only does it help me get more done because I'm not fielding the phone, you know, there's a principle here. If you're on a roll writing copy and you get interrupted for any reason whatsoever for ten minutes, it could take you 20 minutes to get back into the zone, to get back to where you were in terms of momentum and working through the copy. So avoiding any kind of interruption is really important and working at hours when the phone isn't ringing is critical to that. I have a 6.30 call every single morning with the woman that runs the division on the ground there in Jupiter. And so I have two and a half hours of uninterrupted and then I have 15 minutes call with her and then I have another two hours of uninterrupted work in the morning. So I can just pitch a lot of stuff out during that time. This is a pet peeve of mine. When copywriters work with me, I used to tell them that they had to do this or I would not review their copy. And I had a mutiny on my hand so I kind of backed off of that. But I got to tell you, you know, if somebody's used auto format or auto correct or bulleted numbered lists in their copy and I want to change an item in that bulleted or numbered list or I want to change the bullet from a square to a circle to a check mark, you know, something else, or do any other editing with that, if he had this on when he was writing it, it's going to take me a lot longer to deal with fixing it because it just does. And so small things, but it will save you every single time you go into edit a piece, it will save you minutes, you know, in editing. Through the art of the no consequences brain dump. At some point in your process, you're ready to write. You've done all the preparation we've talked about. It's not only on the screen, it's in your head. You've been living with this prospect and with this product for a number of days now. You've got it up here. You've thought about your theme, you've thought about your outline generally what you're going to do. That's the time. Dave and I were just talking about this. It's a time that you'll know that you're ready to write. You're emotionally involved with the product. You like this product. You like your prospect. You know him and you understand him. And you just feel that you're ready. Almost passionate. Instead of being in a procrastination phase where you're going, what excuse can I come up with for not writing today? You're going, I can't wait to get to my desk and see how this starts to flow. And so at that point, I quite often will do this brain dump. We all know that this theory that we've got two people living inside us. We've got our inner adult and we have our inner child. This is the period of time where you just make that inner adult shut the heck up. You need to be able to just dump. You need just every thought that comes into your mind and just let it flow and not judge it is what I'm saying. Not judge it. The inner adult is the guy who looks at everything the inner child does and says, oh, that's immature or that's silly or that'll never work. So you got to get that guy to shut up or you're never going to get the creativity you make. So this is all about speed writing. What I do is I lean back, close my eyes, picture my prospect. It's especially helpful if like Carleen, my prospect is somebody I know, somebody I know that fits the profile. And I just start talking to him as a friend. I talk to him about the problem the product solves. I talk to him about how it does it. I talk to him. I show him, you should see what these people say about this thing. It's really incredible. And you just have this nice conversation with them. And like I said, it goes fast. It just flows out your fingers. Now, how much better is that than what most copywriters do, which is just like they was at Hemingway that said, you just opened a vein and bled all over the page. Writing can be very difficult. But this is fun, it's creative, and you'll get so many great ideas out on the page. Now I do follow my basic outline, whatever it is for that promotion. But also, as I'm writing, I'll think of other ideas. I'll think of other ways to convince my prospect of something. What we're doing is not literature. What we're doing is persuasion. It's sales. So if you have this conversation with your prospect, as if your life depended on selling him this product, it's going to really give you a great head start on your copy. Lower your expectations. Again, this gets back to the adult and child thing. There's a time during your copywriting for the adult to come out to play and to be judgmental. About something that's been done to say this will work, that won't work, to pass judgment on the copy. But forcing yourself into a situation where while you're writing, you're constantly judging the copy. What's going to happen is you're going to get into a type, a part of the copy, and you're not going to like what you're writing. And then the thoughts start coming. We're going to talk about the mindset this afternoon, I think. But the thoughts are coming. I'm no good. The Carleen thoughts, I suck. She used to call me all the time whenever she had, not all the time, I would do nap and then off. But she'd call me when she had a failure and she'd just be yelling, I suck. And I would tell her, look, go get your purse, go to the mall, and buy something nice. And then tomorrow you come back and you go back to work. But you've got to silence that voice inside you. And so there's an appropriate time to be critical. And it's late in the editing process. But while you're putting your first draft on paper, it's really important to lower your expectations. Every sentence doesn't have to be perfect. Every word choice doesn't have to be perfect. The organization, you can play around with it if you want to or you can come back to that later. The idea is to fully develop every selling point. Writing faster is what we're talking about. Discover the magic of XX and Ampersand. No matter how much research you do as you're working through the copy, you're going to come to a point where you, oh my god, I forgot to ask this. Or to get this data point. Or to get this piece of information. So when you're doing that first draft, again, it's about speed writing. This is really getting your emotions, your animal instincts out onto the page. So if you come to something that would stop you and say, oh crap, I've got to go look that up. Don't look it up. Don't look it up. Just say, last year the Federal Reserve printed Ampersand dollars. Or a study by National Institutes at Health says that this drug will XXX, insert side effects. So that allows you to breeze right through it. Keep your momentum going. Instead of constantly, remember, interruption is death if you're trying to write fast. It doesn't matter if it's a phone. It doesn't matter if it's a brick wall you slam into while you're writing copies. So just go right over those on that first draft. Repurpose and restate. I steal from myself all the time. You guys are going to be doing copy for the web as well as direct mail. It's harder to do this in direct mail, although I have to a degree. But when you're doing online copy, typically what you'll do is some part of a campaign, or maybe you're doing the entire campaign. So you'll have something called traffic drivers, which are emails, web ads, and so forth that drive people to a squeeze page or to a sales page. Quite often, and I'll usually write the first of those first, and then turn to the sales page and I say, wait a minute, I've already written this thing once. If I just string all the emails I've written on this subject together, there's a tremendous value there that I can steal. I can steal from myself. And it's okay. One of the things that you learn, the more online promotions you do, is that on any given email you send out, you'll be lucky if 20% of the file reads it, even really looks at it. So the chances are 8 to 2 that if you steal from yourself, your prospect won't recognize the copy. So you can go back and use your own earlier work to speed up the process. There's another thing that I did when I was writing for a lot of direct mail for Weiss Research. Our flagship newsletter was Safe Money Report. I understood that the headline, the deck, and the opening copy, the theme, and the pitch for the premium were really the different thing that we were testing. The product was still the same. So I used the same product copy for three years. I never even looked at my product copy. It was done. It was the best I knew how to write. There it is, right? So then when I had to write a package for them, all I really had to do was think about the front end of the piece. And by the way, during that period of time, we became the largest financial newsletter in the world. We had 120,000 paid subs at $98 a piece. So it worked. So look for opportunities to repurpose and restate stuff you've already written. Have an editing strategy. We're going to talk more about this tomorrow and in a couple of the sessions, I think. But we also covered it earlier today. But it's very important to know, as you're writing it, you know, it'll, remember we talked about letting each piece tell you how it wants to get written. Well, as you're going through, you're going to say, well, this piece is definitely going to be needing some help with word choice or organization or with some other thing. You just start feeling it as you're going through that this is an editing pass I'm really going to have to make on this. So when you get a complete draft, sit down and really look at the piece and say, what do I need to focus on? What editing passes do I need to go through? And I'll typically do five. Once I have a, I call the complete draft, the initial complete draft, a rough draft, because it's a brain dump. It just happened really fast. There wasn't a lot that I really thought about. It's instinct. Nine times out of ten, I find my instincts are right. And so not a lot of changes required. But going through the structured editing is huge. And I did at least five before the client sees it. This is a good one. I deal with copywriters all the time and creating their copy. And I'm reading through it and I'm going, what in the heck is he saying? He just spent a half a page saying nothing. He didn't move the ball. He didn't get me more intrigued. He didn't get me, he didn't persuade me. He's just kind of tap dancing, you know? And whenever you read your own copy and you hit a spot like that, 99% of the time it's going to be because you don't have enough information on that particular point. And so you found yourself tap dancing, trying to fill space. I know I'm supposed to write about this particular aspect. But all of a sudden the copy becomes very vague, very kind of soft and fuzzy. And that's because you don't know enough. And so that's the point of time where you yell help. You go to your client. You say, I don't know enough about this offer. I'm not really clear on this particular part of the theme or the USP. And you talk it out with the client. The quicker you recognize that you need help on a particular part of the copy, the faster the writing process is going to go. It doesn't, absence does not make the heart grow fonder to a client. If you're working on a project, your job is not to avoid impertuning the client with questions. You know, your job is to produce the best copy you know how to produce. So if you have to stay in the client's face asking for additional information along the line, that's perfectly fine. One of my cubs, Ken Komey, my God, the man is a question asking machine. And he asks more questions, you know, before the process begins and then every day he'll be a call from good old Kent, you know, hey Clayton, I'm working on this part of the thing and I just had a question. Give me a call. And you know, it's great because that's just one more thing I'm not going to have to deal with in the creating process. So ask for help early enough and it'll keep things moving for you. This is a big one for me. And I am lucky because I'm married Wendy. She loves direct marketing. She understands direct marketing. And you know, we live kind of separate lives a little bit. You know, she's got two, three houses to maintain. She's got, you know, a lot of work. She works real hard at, you know, taking care of all of us. And she got two kids in college and all this kind of stuff. So meanwhile, I'm down there in the basement in my little cave writing copy. So I'm living in the world of finance and investment and she's living in the real world. So when we go to lunch together, I just love saying, you know, just saying, oh, so I'm working on this thing now that the newsletter is about technology investing. And, you know, I've got some problems with it. You know, one of them is everyone I'm going to lost money in 2000 when the tech stocks wrecked. And I don't really know how to deal with that. And just there are two things that happen. Number one, Wendy will quite often come up with a solution or a suggestion. Quite often they're very usable. Sometimes it's an absolute solution. But also by hearing myself state what the situation is, what I'm struggling with, or what the USP is, you know, other things will come to me and they might be additive ideas. They might be a better way to do what I'm doing. And it really, really helps. And so if you have a spouse or a friend who's willing to listen to you, prattle on about what you're writing about, that is really valuable. Just being able to acknowledge, once you start talking, you're going to say things that surprise you about problems you're having, the package that you may or may not have even been aware of at the time. Yes, microphone. I would just add to that too that not only that, but try to sell the person. Don't just say, oh, I can figure out how to sell this and I don't know maybe this strategy or that strategy, but just say, you know, I'm working on this book and it's really exciting because it's this book about herbal medicine. And herbal medicine is really cool for this reason. This book is really cool for this reason. And see if the person's eyes light up. Or, you know, do, what does it take to make that person get excited about what you're doing? Especially good if they're not really into that because now you've got an uphill process or challenge. Yeah. I think that's really good. Say what? I didn't hear that. I said, this is how David and I do our crits, basically, is I get all excited about a headline idea and I call him and I say, this is going to work. And he's like, I don't know, it's kind of boring. It's not really doing it for me. I'm like, are you kidding me? And by the time I have to finish arguing with him 30 minutes later, usually what happens is it's not my original idea. It's been tempered and refined. And I finally found the phrase that he goes, well, when you put it that way, it sounds good. Yeah. Well, we have something at Weiss. It's called the Martin Factor. Martin is the most negative human being I have met in my entire life. The man, I tried to write copy one time and got and overthought it so much and debunked all of his own sales arguments in his own mind that he was paralyzed. And he said, we can't do this promotion. We can. This is just baloney. This is baloney, right? So I said, I'll write it for you. And it took me like 30 minutes. So we call it the Martin Factor and it's on the schedule. It's like Clayton writes first draft, Martin craps all over it, Clayton writes second draft, Martin likes it a little more, Clayton writes final draft, we limited him to three drafts. He gets to see two. The third draft goes to the compliance department. But it's a Martin Factor. But the Martin Factor is what's made me so successful at Weiss, I think. Because if you're a copywriter, if you haven't read my article, For Sale Dead Puppies, you need to read it. It's about this. When you're writing copy, you need to keep a positive mindset. You're positive about the product. You're upbeat. You're excited about it, right? So you're selling yourself on the product as you're writing this copy, right? So what you really need at some point in the process is a Martin. You need somebody to come in and burst your bubble. And to point out every flaw in your argument. And that's what this Talking It Out thing does, as David was pointing out. And Wendy does this all the time. I remember I came to her, I said, I'm doing a promotion for Y2K, right? And she goes, oh man, it's not going to happen. It's not real. It's hoax. It's crazy. And I talked her into the fact that it was going to happen. It was real. And the entire country was going to fall apart. And it never happened. But that process allowed me to write some really good promotions in the meantime. And we brought in 40,000 paid subs on that. And believe it or not, they converted to full-time subs after Y2K passed. But anyway, having that negative point of view, you're trying to be positive. Having somebody who's more skeptical is very, very helpful. Read it out loud. I will think that something is the slickest piece of copy I ever wrote, that I've added it, polished it to a fine sheen. And then I read it out loud. And there's missing words. There's phrases that make no sense. There's entire paragraphs that are clusters. And reading it out loud really, really helps you. It really helps you identify those rough patches in the copy. All right. So this is me playing amateur psychologist. But you got the good angel and the bad angel. You got the adult and child. Well, you have the right brain and left brain. And the right brain is the creative side. And the left brain is the accounting mentality. And it's all detail-oriented and all that kind of stuff. So what happens is you get up in the morning and you go to work. Now, this is about getting more work done every day. I asked a bunch of copywriters, how many hours a day can you write copy? I mean, intensive copywriting. And the answer I keep getting is maybe five or six. And then I'm exhausted. I can put in 12, 14 hours without breaking a sweat and feel as fresh when I'm not. That's really overstating it. But I feel pretty good at the end of the day anyway. And the way that I do it is I'll get up in the morning and I'll take my creative tasks first, right? Anything that requires right brain. Everything that has to do with putting two ideas together in a way that they've never been put together before. Anything that has to do with a headline that will really stop people. Anything that has to do with creativity of any kind. I'll work on that side of the brain for as long as I can. And it might only be five or six hours. But then when I feel myself flagging, I'm losing it. I'm feeling tired. I take a break. I'll go upstairs and say hi to Wendy. Grab a piece of cheese. And I'll do until recently. And then go back down to my desk and I'll open up a document that requires me to crunch numbers. Or do something left brain. Maybe I'll be parsing an editor's track record. Or I'll be looking at the makeup of a particular vitamin or a formula or something. But it's left brain stuff that leaves my right brain alone. I don't have to be creative at all. It might be reorganizing research. It might be looking back at my outline. Maybe reorganizing that. That's a logical process. It's not necessarily a creative one. So now, whereas most writers are taking the rest of the day off, I'm getting another 50% out of my day, maybe, by taking care of the left brain stuff when my right brain is exhausted. And give it a try. I mean, it really works for me. I think it'll work good for you too. Creative loathing. I had a note on this, but this is more than just the Edison kind of approach. The bottom line is you have to take time off. You have to take time off on weekends, evenings, time with the family, time with the kids. You're going to be getting away from the desk and so forth. And the idea here is very similar to the Edison one now. It's always leave to go do whatever you're going to do with something in your mind about the project you're working on. Maybe it's a question that you have about a particular way to approach something. Maybe it's whatever, a challenge that you're dealing with a copy or something that just doesn't feel right in the copy. Maybe you're not persuaded or convinced of a certain point. And leave with that. Then you go to the mall, go hang out at the beach, take the kids to the park, whatever you're doing. Even if you don't consciously think about it, again, it's that scrabble break, right? You've gotten away from the scrabble board, your brain's still going to chew on it and it's going to serve up an answer. So, you've done all of the above. You've sped through your copy in record time. You're feeling really good about it. You've done like five editing passes, but now you face that fateful moment when sooner or later the client's got to see it, right? A lot of writers will procrastinate at that point. They'll say, well, I'll sleep on it another couple of nights. I'm not really happy with this particular passage. The fact is that the two wonderful things happen when you just turn the copy in. Three things happen. The first thing is you have a better shot at coming in close to your deadline, which makes clients very, very happy. A second thing that happens is it allows you to be free for a short period of time without feeling any deadline pressure, any pressure at all to work on this job. So now it's in the client's court. It's not my problem anymore, right? So that gives you a lot of that separation that really helps with creativity. The third thing it does is the client is going to give you a fresh perspective on it, not just the same one you'd get by showing it to strangers, not just the one that you would get by reading it to your spouse. The client's going to give you an informed opinion on, not the copy as a whole typically, but on the passages, various passages in the copy. It's an informed opinion typically based on testing. Well, I went to Marty Edelson and I said, this is insane. You guys never have a toll free number in any of your Magilocks. This is just crazy. They had to return this, it was a soft offer. You had to return this little postcard. You couldn't just call and say, yeah, I'll take my free book. You had to send in this postcard. And I said, this is insane. Adding a phone number for all of the instant gratification people, adding a phone number would be fantastic. It's exactly what's needed. He said, no, we tested it. We've tested it every way we could think of. We agreed with you. But the fact is, when we tested it, the market told us, nope, just use the BRC, the business reply card. So I wouldn't have known that had Marty not been creating my copy because I had put the toll free number in. So he had an informed opinion. I was guessing. So you're going to get a lot of good input from clients. And I keep trying not to steal from tomorrow. But at some point, we're going to talk about how to handle crits. There's like six kinds of crits. And the ways that you address those crits will really go a long way to determining the ultimate success or failure of the package. Now I talked about this before a couple of times. I don't have to dwell on it. But if you're in any of this process, and it doesn't fit with the project you're working on, change your process. It's not like we're all Baptist and we have to do it just this one way, or pick your religion, or political party. We don't have to all do it this way. Each project is going to be different in some regard, some radically different. So have some flexibility. You're only as good as your tools. I've got two Apple computers, two iMacs. And I've got my main computer is a PC. For me, the iMacs are problematic. The PCs work much better for me. And it's not just that when mailed DAP thing that they have going on. There are other issues as well. So find what works for you. Now think about the tools you use. And really go home and look at your setup. What kind of mouse are you using? Do you have a programmable mouse that allows you to automate certain procedures when you're writing? Wouldn't it be cool to be able to highlight a passage of copy and with just one click of the mouse change it from this face to that face? Or to automatically drop in a bullet? There are little scripts that you can write within these programs that will do all of that for you. So look at your setup, focus on it. Is it fast enough? Is it modern enough? Do you have the software you need? One of the things that I ran into, and I still run into quite often, is that the stuff I do often requires some kind of a mock-up, like Carlene was talking about. And that means that I've got to have some way of showing the client what it's going to look like ultimately. And that quite often means I have to edit photographs. In some cases, it means I need to do just a little video edit. Nothing complicated. I'll let somebody else do the complicated stuff. Maybe I want to pick up something somebody said at a certain point on a video. Having Photoshop and understanding how to use it and having a good video editor and how to use it ultimately saves me time because it means I don't have to do some hand-drawn exotic, this is how I want it, or have it designed totally wrong and have it redone. So make a list of the things that you think would help you. There's a program, and we're doing more and more video. The internet's going to video. So there's a program called Enounce. It's a good case in point. We were talking about this today. Let's say you have a video on your desktop. You take my advice to get Jeff Walker's course on PLF, which we'll talk about tomorrow. And so you've got like a hundred videos that you want to watch. You can watch them, and Enounce will let you speed them up. So you can watch the video, get everything he's saying in half the time by doubling the speed. And I find out quite often that you can even go faster that and still get everything he's saying. And it's really helpful when you're listening to some guru like me who's long-winded. You just want him to get to the point. So yeah, Enounce is a good case in point. It saves you tons of time. Another thing, this doesn't come in the area of tools, but I demand that my clients, if I'm working with video, I demand that my clients give me a transcript of the video. Working with the video itself is a nightmare. But if I have a transcript, I can do word searches, and I can find the sections I'm looking for, and I can edit it, change tracking on very easily and quickly. And I don't have to watch an hour-long video. I've got it all right there. So another really in that area is that one of the best investments I ever made was a second monitor for my computer. How many of you guys use two or more monitors? Yeah. I had one point. I had three of the big Apple Cinema displays, the big ones. It looked like I was in Mission Central at NASA or something. It looked like I could launch missiles at Moscow or something from back there. But what it allowed me to do is I kept my email open over here, maybe a Skype window or a Messenger window. Then on these two, I could copy out of documents here and paste over here. I didn't have to resize documents or anything. Sounds like a small thing. This is about saving time, doing it faster. It's definitely worth the investment if you don't already have one. Speaking of which, Skype. My God. I'm amazed at how few people use it. How many of you guys use Skype? Okay. Probably half of you. It's like being in your client's office and being able to shout over the divider between your desks. I'm typing along and I go, I forgot. What's the pricing on this again? I go, hey, Jen, what's the pricing on this again? She goes, 149 bucks. I go, okay, 149 bucks. Done. No phone call needed. No conversation. I got the information instantly and I was able to keep up my momentum. It amazes me that so few people have it. I hire copywriters all the time. They don't have it. So get it. I Skype you from the house just upstairs. Yeah. I'm in the house. Martin has a house in Brazil and we work about the same hours. I'll just type in and I said, ping me when you're up. He'll get up, he'll get to his computer. I'm up. What do you want to talk about it? I just hit call. All of a sudden there's little old Martin and his pajamas. We have our conversation and solve whatever problem is required. Okay, Dropbox, when I sent the PowerPoints for this weekend to Denise, they would not have fit in an email as attachments in an email. I could have used Hytale, which will zip a file and send high volume files. Like an FTP download kind of thing. But Dropbox was so much easier. I just copied everything, dropped it into Dropbox, right click on the file folder, you go down to share link and then paste that into an email sent off to Denise and all of a sudden she's got everything she needs. So there's no upload time, there's no sitting and waiting or watching or anything like that. Just saves you a lot of time. Go to my PC helps me a lot because I have so many houses and also when I'm on the road I like to be able to go to my other computers and pull documents and stuff if I have work to do. I use Big Stock Photo for all of these pictures are from Big Stock. It's fairly cheap. It's a very easily searchable database of photographs. We talked about the handgun headline that we did for that health company. Having access to this kind of database you can just get online and just start looking for stuff and you'll see a photograph and you go, oh that's a headline. Sometimes you can do that, see an incredible picture and then write a headline around it. Those are the services I use most often. The other thing about this in terms of time savers is I have two browsers that I use. I have my Apple browser and I have Internet Explorer and I use Internet Explorer only for work related items. So in my menu bar across the top I have every website that I need in order to get a quick number or whatever. So I've got Bloomberg. I've got Kitco for gold and silver. I've got Big Charts for stocks. All the sites that I've identified that help me write copy. They help me get additional research quick outside of what I've acquired from my client's research department. So if you're writing health packages it might be National Institutes of Health, WebMD, whatever you use for research. And that browser is dedicated to that. So all those sites are right there and it's just one click and you're there and you get the answer. So that's my 20 ways. Let's talk. You have any questions? Writers, any other ideas, any other suggestions for saving time? What do you like about the Mac versus the PC? I don't. I like the PC better. What do you like about the Mac versus the PC? Yeah, I like the PC better. I like the Mac. I've always had performance problems with Macs. I've had them crash on me. They say Macs never crash. They just unexpectedly quit. So that was a line of bull. For me, Macs are the way to go if you're an artist. Definitely the way to go if you're a graphic artist, designer. If you're editing film, definitely the way to go. For simple word processing, I just find Microsoft Word the way to go. I like running it on a PC rather than on a Mac just because of the interconnectivity and all of that other stuff. I get tired of dealing with windmill dat files. I know there are programs that will handle that, but how in the world can you sell a computer that won't give you an email attachment? Part of it is my bias. I mean, that's what I started with. Actually, I started with an Apple. I had an Apple II. That was my very first computer. Anybody ever work with one of those? Oh, my God. Okay, so you're typing copy and you've got this green screen with black letters on it or black letters with black screen with green letters. You're typing along and you go, okay, I want to move that paragraph from here to over here. So you type in a code. This is before a mouse was invented. You typed in a code and that highlighted the paragraph. And then the thing would say, please insert disk 2. And so you had these big five-inch floppies. So then you'd insert disk 2. It would go whirr, whirr, whirr, whirr, whirr. And then it would say, please insert disk 5. And so you would do that and the same thing. So now it's ready and you type in the code for cut. And then the same process for paste. And so cutting and pasting a paragraph could take you three or four minutes. But then I come from the time when a fax machine took, what was it? The equip machine was like six minutes to transmit one page and it's stunk. Do you guys know Carbonite? Basically you install it and every time you create a document, it automatically sends it up to the cloud. And every time you change document, it sends it up to the cloud. So you can be anywhere on planet Earth access your Carbonite account online. You don't need it even to have a computer with the app on it. Access Carbonite online and you can access every document on your own computer. The other strategy I have is probably because of many years being a newspaper editor, so I mean you have to have stuff that's going out. I would email things to myself to lay out of account photos, what have you, so that Carbonite went down, I can still recreate something or I will email it. Well, it's Murphy's Law. In preparation for this weekend, I sent Denise the dropped out box link. I put everything on a flash drive. I transferred everything on that flash drive to a laptop. And so I had like, and then I emailed everything to myself. So I've got backups for backups for backups and believe it or not, I've still come into situations where I couldn't access a file when I needed it. So you just can't be too careful. Yes? I have a question about something you said a little earlier. Sure. When you started a new project, do you ever use a creative brief, like send it to the client? Because one of the copywriting teachers out there says always use that. Well, I was talking earlier about a copy platform or a copy outline. And that's basically what it is. We don't have a particular formula that we use for creating those because I work for, I got 16 freelancers that I work with. Plus I've got two in-house copywriters. And each one of them has their own style, their own way of thinking and so forth. So we don't have a standard format for those things. But we have certain requirements that we ask that they fulfill in creating this. And the copy platform, again, is typically the headline deck, opening copy, and then an outline of how they anticipate the package to go. Now everybody knows that once you're into it, it's like no plan survives the first shot, no battle plan survives the first shot. So it's understood by everybody that this is the plan, but the copywriter has full freedom as he's working through the material to change it. So, absolutely, sure. Chris, where's Chris? You've got a challenger here. The client sends you a product that doesn't have a clear USP, it's not clear to you. And they say it's your job to find it. Is that what you're supposed to be doing or is the client supposed to have a USP for their product? Well, I'd give it a fair run. There are a lot of clients that really don't know what USP is. They know they're three letters, but they're really not sure what they mean. But they know they need one. And so as copywriters, we're pretty much steeped in that kind of thing. So it doesn't necessarily take that long. And I don't think they're really asking for a verbatim USP. They're not typically asking you to write when it absolutely, positively has to be there overnight, which is the FedEx USP. What they're asking is, what is the unique selling proposition? And you might state that same USP by saying reliable overnight delivery. And that's enough. And you can get fancy with it or put it in the pros when you're writing the copy. If nothing stands up to you as this product being wildly different from many other products out there, do you try to create that? Or do you try to dig deep and say to them, well, when you were writing this, what made you think that this was different? Well, if I look at a product and I can't figure out one unique thing about that product, and the client can't figure out one unique thing about that product, makes it a selling proposition, it's gonna seriously impact my decision whether to take the job or not. Now, remember every time you say yes, you're spending a certain percentage of your year. And so, let's say it takes you a month to write a package, and in the old direct mail days it did. So you have 12 packages max that you can write, and that's if you take no vacations, right? So 12 max, well, for every one of those that doesn't return a royalty to you, the cost to you is horrendous, right? So being picky about the projects you accept is really important. Now in the early going, I know anything you can do to put samples in your portfolio is a great idea, right? But you need to acknowledge, and we'll be talking about long money versus short money and so forth tomorrow, but you need to acknowledge the idea that when you commit to doing a package, you're giving up a piece of your life to that package. And if it doesn't give you what you need to sell it successfully, I would walk. Unless you just need the advance to live on. Which- At this point I do, because I'm starting in this, so I need- Sure, a lot of people do. Yeah, I did too when I was starting out. So in that case, you do the best you can. Now the classic idea for a USP, a unique selling proposition, remember what Hopkins wrote about, okay? Let's see, do I have Hopkins here? No, I don't. So Hopkins was trying to sell beer, right? Are you familiar with the story? Yeah, I am. I know the story. So basically, for anyone who doesn't know the story, they said, we want you to sell beer. He says, well, geez, why would I buy this beer instead of these other beers? It tastes the same, it comes in the same kind of container. Why would I want to do it? So he goes to the brewery and takes a tour of the brewery and asks a lot of questions. He finds out that there's a certain part of the process where water is involved, I forget what it was. But I think it's the steam clean, I forget what it was. Anyway, every brewer did it. But Hopkins looks at this and goes, yeah, every brewer does it, but the consumer doesn't know that. So if I build my campaign on that, remember we talked about the top rung on the ladder? Nobody could compete with Hopkins beer ads on that basis because he was the top line on the ladder because Miller or whatever it was did this one thing with their brewing process. And it didn't matter that everyone else did it too. So you might look at the product in that way. What can I say about this that's new and different in the positions that it's being unique even though it may not be? Sure. Anybody else? Coming back to when you talked about repurposing and restating copy. If I understood you correctly, you said that there were times when you used essentially all of the proof and the credibility and the guarantee. You kept that the same and you just would come up with a new headline and lead and send that out as a new promotion? Pretty much, yeah. The thing is that my rationale on that, and of course when I started doing that, nobody else was. They were writing everything from scratch on every package. And my rationale was first of all, if I do it that way, I can probably write a package in half the time. Secondly, the back end is proven to work because I'm taking it from a control. Thirdly, the people who reply or react to this lead, this headline, this opening have probably never seen this ending. They've gotten other promotions with other leads but didn't respond to them by definition. I'm mailing to prospects. So very few of them have probably ever even gotten to the point where they saw the offer. We were able to get away with it for years at a time. It's a good idea though when you do that to revisit that copy maybe once a year because it's amazing. It's like the game of gossip. Every time you reuse it, some little thing will get dropped or some sentence will go away or whatever. So it's good to review it and to make sure that it's still all there. I actually got nailed for that once. I hadn't looked at the back end copy for a year and when I did it was just like nothing like what I had written originally. What reading program do you use when you're doing your first draft all the way through Word? Word. Okay. Never Scrivener or anything. I'm playing vanilla. Word does it all for me. I just turn everything off. Every time Bill Gates tries to mess around with Mike, tries to help me. The little paper clip jumps up and says, excuse me, how can I screw up your copy today? I turn all that crap off and once that's done, I'm really fast with it. I don't know if it would help but online I write in Verdana, Verdana 12, body text, offline. If I'm writing a direct mail piece, I'll typically write it in New Times, Roman. Eventually an offline piece will probably be set in New Times and it gives you a better idea of the length and where everything fits. Online is getting better. What's happening right now is we're finding out that in the old days, a serif typeface has all the little curly cues and stuff on the letters. In the old days, screen resolution was so crappy that the dots on your screen were as big as the curly cues and it was very hard to read. Everybody went to a serif typeface like Ariel and Verdana. Verdana is a little bigger than Ariel given the same point size. Everybody was using Ariel and Verdana and other serif faces. But now we have these incredibly high resolution monitors. I'm starting to use serif faces, 18 point, Constantia or New Times, Roman or another serif face at 18 points or larger for subheads and deck copy and stuff like that. I think most of us who are prospects are older. They're going to be in their 50s and 60s. They were reading newspapers and books that were all printed in a serif face. They're very comfortable with that. If we can get there online, I think that would be a good move. Later on, the next generation who grew up on computers, they grew up with sans serif faces. That will probably change. I use a serif face whenever I can. Do you ever use all caps and subheads or do you try to avoid that? All caps? Yeah, all cap subheads. I won't even initial cap a headline if I can avoid it. I think it makes it harder to read. I can't avoid it. Using all caps for anything other than just one word for emphasis, I think is, maybe it's a personal choice, but my suspicion is that it hurts readership. Yes, Chris? Quick question and a comment too, but when I write sometimes and I find I'm stuck and like I've just lost the flow, I will sometimes say, I'll write it. I'll end up with about three or four sentences on one thing and then I just keep going. Then I go back and go, oh, that's the one. I don't know if anybody else ever does that, but I do do that sometimes. For me, it seems to work. My quick question, Clayton, you mentioned some of the sites like you have up across your browser for the financial. Was it Kitko? Kitko. Kitko. KIT. KIT. Big charts? Big charts is really good for stocks. You want to know, I'm always in a situation where I have to write about what I did this this morning, six o'clock. I have to get up and write an email for the file. I have a unique problem. This is Murphy's log in. I'm selling a tech stock investing service. In the middle of a campaign, it's been six weeks so far. We only launched the product a couple of days ago. We're writing a heat of collecting money for it. Guess what happened to tech stocks this week? Yeah. NASDAQ just bloody, ugly crash. Of course, we were going on, one of our themes was tech stocks are in this new great bull market. If you're not in, you're crazy. Take this gun, shoot yourself. Right in the middle of all of that. How do you recover from that? It struck me that it might be the case that even though the NASDAQ fell 100 points yesterday, that that was the Apple's, Microsoft's, and Amazon's of the world, and that the small kinds of tech stocks that my client recommends may have done better than that. So USA Today has a most active page. You can go there. You just Google most active. That'll be the first thing that comes up. USA Today's most active page. So I went there and I put in NYSE and greatest percent gain and clicked Find Stocks. It fed me up, I think it's 50 stocks that gained the most yesterday. I just scrolled up through that list and picked out the tech stocks. It turns out that while the NASDAQ, tech heavy NASDAQ fell 100 and some odd points, that there were individual stocks, the small techs, the new superstars that went up as much as 28%. So that allowed me then to do an email yesterday morning and a similar one this morning where I basically listed all of the small stocks that we follow in the service that gained, not all of them, but like 10 or 12 of them. And of course made the point, this is just sampling. So. And just further to that point, because that can happen, especially I think in financial, but could possibly happen in other areas too. You can, it's probably best to go to say to your client, look, I know I did these launch email or whatever emails, it's changed and kind of preempt, do a preemptive action with that then. Yeah. You were actually asking about Big Stock when I jumped off on that, but Big, Big, Big, yeah, Big Stock. Oh, it's Big Stock or Big Charts? Big Charts, Big Charts. There's Big Stock photo too, but no, on Big Charts, what you do there is you're writing an email and you go, gee, I wonder what happened to the S&P today? Is it up? Is it down? Is it sideways? Or you need a quick piece of information on an individual stock. What's it selling for now? I had to do this promotion on Berkshire Hathaway and it was selling for $280,000 for one share of stock. This is the Berkshire Hathaway A stock. And so I needed a chart for Berkshire Hathaway. And again, I will not go get a Bloomberg terminal. I had one for a year and never used it. It's just mind boggling. And so I was able to just look at Berkshire Hathaway A stock, right click, copy a chart off of Big Charts, drop it in my email, and then I, of course, instructed the graphics department they had to recreate the chart, but here's the line, the shape, and it's a great, great site to have. And you can do smart charts on it. You can ask for different parameters like moving averages and all kinds of other stuff with it. So it's a great site. All right, one minute, guys. Last shot. Okay. Great. Well, we went three minutes into the lunch hour, so let's go eat. Great.
